src/main.py:
Starts the graphical maze app and puts `src` on `sys.path` so `gui` imports work.
Parses an optional maze file from the command line and hands it to `gui.run_gui`.
Run from the `MazeGame` folder: `py src/main.py` or `py src/main.py -f examples/level2.txt`.
src/text_game.py:
Terminal version of the maze: WASD moves, `p` shows a shortest path, `q` quits.
Loads levels with `maze_load`, moves with `maze_play`, and prints via `text_display`.
Optional first argument selects which `.txt` level to open.
src/pathfinding.py:
Breadth-first search that returns a shortest cell path or `None` if the goal is unreachable.
Uses `maze_play.is_walkable` so walls (`#`) block expansion like in the rest of the game.
Fine for small mazes; huge grids would use parent pointers instead of storing full paths in the queue.
src/maze_load.py:
Reads a maze text file into `list[list[str]]` and enforces a rectangle with one `S` and one `G`.
Blank lines are skipped; every non-empty line becomes one row of characters.
Raises `ValueError` if the file is empty, ragged, or missing start/goal markers.
src/maze_play.py:
Defines walkability and `move_player` for one step in `directions.UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT`.
The grid from disk is not edited when the player walks; only coordinates change.
`check_win` compares player position to the goal cell.
src/grid.py:
Shared maze alphabet (`#`, `.`, `S`, `G`) and helpers: bounds, copying, path overlay for text view.
Row 0 is the top of the file; columns grow to the right like the canvas.
`overlay_path` builds a display grid with path hints and a `P` for the player without mutating the original.
src/directions.py:
String direction names and `(d_row, d_col)` deltas used by both the GUI key map and `move_player`.
Keeps movement vocabulary separate from Tk keysyms like `Up`.
Must stay consistent with `gui.constants.KEY_TO_DIR`.
src/text_display.py:
Formats grids for printing and merges player (and optional path) onto a copy for the terminal UI.
`default_level_path` points at the bundled `examples/level1.txt`.
`print_help` shows the one-screen control summary at startup.
src/gui/__init__.py:
Tkinter package entry: ensures the parent `src` directory is on `sys.path` for sibling imports.
Exports `run_gui`, which builds the root window and starts `MazeNavigatorApp`.
Import as `gui` after running `main.py` or any launcher that adds `src` to the path.
src/gui/__main__.py:
Allows `py -m gui` with optional `--file` / `-f` for a maze path.
Missing files fall back to the welcome screen instead of exiting with an error.
Delegates to `run_gui` after argparse.
src/gui/app.py:
Owns the Tk root and swaps between the welcome screen and an in-game `MazeGameFrame`.
Tracks which campaign levels are unlocked and completed.
Resolves bundled level paths through `levels.level_path`.
src/gui/constants.py:
Colours, cell size, window geometry, and `KEY_TO_DIR` for the Tk front end.
No parsing or movement rules—only presentation tokens and welcome-screen copy.
Edit `INSTRUCTIONS_TEXT` to change what new players read on the menu.
src/gui/levels.py:
Maps level numbers 1–8 to `examples/levelN.txt` paths using `__file__` so assets resolve reliably.
`level_num_from_path` recognises campaign filenames for “next level” behaviour.
Custom maze paths still load; they simply skip automatic progression metadata.
src/gui/maze_frame.py:
One playable level: keyboard input, canvas redraws, reset/path keys, and win callbacks.
Uses `maze_load` / `maze_play` plus `pathfinding` for the optional yellow path overlay.
Bumps the canvas with `vibration.shake_canvas` when a move hits a wall.
src/gui/maze_play_ui.py:
Builds the in-game layout: header, canvas, status label, and control buttons.
Returns a small dataclass of widgets so `MazeGameFrame` stays readable.
All behaviour is passed in as callbacks; this module only packs Tk widgets.
src/gui/maze_render.py:
Draws walls, floor, start, goal, optional path tint, and the player on the maze canvas.
Called after `canvas.delete("all")` so each frame is a full repaint.
Maps file characters to fill colours from `constants`.
src/gui/player.py:
Turns `(row, col)` into pixel rectangles for `cell_rect` and `draw_player` on the canvas.
Uses `CELL_PX` and an inset so the avatar reads clearly on any tile type.
Coordinate system matches the text maze files (origin top-left, rows down).
src/gui/styles.py:
Applies shared primary (blue) and secondary (grey) button styles used across screens.
Centralises colours and padding so welcome and play views stay consistent.
Pairs with `constants.UI_BTN_*` tokens.
src/gui/vibration.py:
Schedules a short horizontal shake on the maze canvas after an illegal move.
Uses a generation counter so rapid repeated bumps do not stack broken animations.
Only the canvas shifts; chrome around it stays still.
src/gui/welcome.py:
Welcome `Frame`: level grid, embedded help text, and quit wired to app callbacks.
Delegates packing details to `welcome_ui` helpers.
Receives unlock and completion sets to style each level button.
src/gui/welcome_ui.py:
Layout helpers for the welcome card: shell, level buttons, scrollable instructions, quit row.
Uses default-argument lambdas so each level button captures the correct index.
No maze rules—pure Tk geometry and styling.
docs/docs.py:
CLI that prints a fixed 2–3 line summary for a project file from `docs/file_summaries.json`.
Resolves paths under the `MazeGame` root; add or edit JSON entries for new files.
Does not read source comments or docstrings.
tests/test_pathfinding.py:
`unittest` cases for `find_path`: corridors, blocked goals, start equals goal, walkable paths.
Also loads `examples/level1.txt` to ensure the real file stays solvable.
Prepends `src` to `sys.path` before importing project modules.
tests/test_movement.py:
`unittest` cases for `move_player`, `is_walkable`, and `load_grid` validation rules.
Uses temp files for invalid mazes and scans `examples/level1.txt`–`level8.txt` exist and load.
Imports direction constants from `directions` and logic from `maze_load` / `maze_play`.
examples/level1.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #1: introductory layout with a clear route from `S` to `G`.
ASCII grid: `#` walls, `.` floor; used by tests as the canonical on-disk example.
Rows are literal maze data—descriptions for `docs.py` live only in `file_summaries.json`.
examples/level2.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #2: same outer size as level 1 with a denser interior wall pattern.
Good for exercising path preview when corridors get narrower.
Loaded like any other level file through `maze_load.load_grid`.
examples/level3.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #3: mid-difficulty layout in the eight-level sequence.
Part of the loop that `test_movement` checks can all be parsed.
Pair with the GUI campaign to unlock later stages after wins.
examples/level4.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #4: continues ramping layout complexity through the campaign.
Solvable like every shipped example; one `S` and one `G` required.
Open with `py src/main.py -f examples/level4.txt` to jump straight in.
examples/level5.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #5: mid-campaign checkpoint in the bundled `examples` set.
Filename pattern `levelN.txt` drives “next level” behaviour in the app.
Same character set as every other level file.
examples/level6.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #6: tighter patterns before the final two levels.
Useful for manual play-testing of shake, win text, and redraw performance.
Not parsed as comments—only as maze rows.
examples/level7.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #7: near-final difficulty in the default eight-level arc.
Still a plain rectangle of characters on disk.
Kept next to other examples for `levels.level_path`.
examples/level8.txt:
Bundled campaign maze #8: last default level with a tall, nested corridor structure.
After a win here the UI shows final-completion messaging instead of “next”.
Same load rules as level 1—rectangle, single start, single goal.
